name: Scene Video Prompt Binary
description: Binary yes/no evaluation of scene_video_prompt JSON quality — the shot breakdown that drives all downstream image and video generation.

questions.question: Do the shots collectively cover the full scene description — every key action, event, and beat from the scene is represented in at least one shot? No significant moments are skipped.
Do the shots use at least 3 different shot types (e.g., wide, medium, close_up, extreme_close_up, tracking, over_shoulder, pov)? Not all the same type.
Does every shot that depicts a character correctly list them in the characters array using the exact character item IDs? No shots that show a character but have an empty characters array.
Does every shot specify the correct setting item ID that matches the location shown? No null settings when the shot clearly takes place in a named location.
Do the individual shot durations sum approximately to the totalDuration field (within ±20%)? No wildly mismatched totals.
Does every shot description contain specific, visual details — what a camera physically sees in this frozen moment? No vague descriptions like 'the scene continues' or 'characters interact'.
Does every shot include a cameraWork field with specific camera direction (angle, movement, framing, depth of field)? No empty or generic camera directions.
Do the shots flow logically from one to the next — is there a clear visual sequence that a video editor could assemble without confusion? No random jumps in location or time without motivation.
Do the shots collectively build an emotional arc across the scene — tension rises, mood shifts, something changes from the first shot to the last?

Does the shot breakdown include at least one character-free shot — an insert, cutaway, or establishing shot showing environment, objects, or atmosphere? Answer YES if present, or if the scene's rapid pacing genuinely wouldn't benefit from one.
Comparing to the provided scene description: do the shots accurately represent the scene's events, characters, and details without inventing new elements or contradicting the scene?

Do the shot descriptions include sound/audio cues — either explicitly named sounds, dialogue references, or ambient audio? At least half the shots should reference what is heard, not just what is seen.

Does each shot have a sensible generationStrategy? Character-free shots (establishing, atmosphere, inserts) should be 't2v'. Shots with characters visible from the start should be 'i2v'. Shots where characters enter mid-shot should be 'i2v_late_entry'. Is there at least one 't2v' or 'i2v_late_entry' shot in the scene (not all shots should be 'i2v')?

Do shots that need visual anchoring (long shots 6s+, shots with significant visual change, shots that chain into the next) include a lastFrame description? And do all shots have a firstFrame? The lastFrame should be omitted for short static shots.
